petitioner_background: The petitioner is a pianist, primarily working as an accompanist, seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the arts under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act. She received her Master of Music Degree from the Cleveland Institute of Music (CIM) in 1994 and attended the New England Conservatory from 1998-2000. She has performed at venues including Weill Recital Hall at Carnegie Hall and the Pacific Music Festival in Sapporo, Japan, and teaches at MIT. She has accompanied various artists and collaborated with members of major symphony orchestras.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) a 1993 two-paragraph article in the New York Times and a press release from the Leschetizky Association reporting she won first prize at the Leschetizky Association's 14th New York Debut Competition (open to pianists age 18-30 preparing for NY debut); (2) 1990 prize at the Priscilla Morneault Piano Competition sponsored by Portland Symphony Orchestra (PSO), with first prize of $2,500 and a PSO performance contract, covered in an unidentified newspaper and the Portland, Maine Evening Express; (3) a May 13, 1994 letter from the CIM President congratulating her for winning the Rosa Lobe Memorial Award from the Cleveland Heights Alumni Chapter of Mu Phi Epsilon and the Arthur Loesser Prize from CIM; (4) 1991 competition at the William S. Boyd Piano Competition at Augusta College in Georgia (no evidence of placement); (5) sixth prize at the 1991 Cleveland International Piano Competition (one of four solo piano competitions in the US recognized by the International Federation of World Music Competitions in Geneva); (6) scholarship to attend New England Conservatory from 1998-2000; (7) letter from an associate principal clarinetist for the Boston Symphony Orchestra asserting the prizes are 'prestigious'; (8) letter claiming the Arthur Loesser Prize is awarded to top 1.33% of applicants, Rosa Lobe to 5%, Leschetizky to 2%, Robert Casadesus International Piano Competition to 1%, and Priscilla Morneault to 2%.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the petitioner failed this criterion. Scholarships are academic in nature and limited to students, so the most experienced and renowned members of the field do not compete for them. The CIM awards (Arthur Loesser Prize, Rosa Lobe Memorial Award) are limited to students at that institute and cannot serve as evidence of awards indicative of national or international acclaim. The Leschetizky Competition is limited to pianists age 18-30 preparing for NY debut, excluding the most experienced and renowned pianists. The record lacks evidence of requirements to enter the Priscilla Morneault Competition, which received only limited local Portland coverage. The record does not establish how petitioner finished at the William S. Boyd Competition, and a sixth place finish at the Cleveland International Piano Competition is not a prize or award. The percentages cited by the reference letter are meaningless without information about the pool of competitors, as finishing in the top 1.33% of CIM students says nothing about position in the field generally. Even if the 1993 Leschetizky first place finish were considered a nationally recognized award, this single award is not evidence of sustained national or international acclaim in 2007 when the petition was filed, as the record contains no prizes or awards other than student scholarships after 1994.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel initially asserted petitioner was submitting evidence of 'recognition from international experts that the alien is a member of associations, which require outstanding achievements of their members.' Counsel referenced letters describing whom the petitioner has accompanied, festivals where she performed, and general assertions of talent.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: None of the letters reference an association of which the petitioner is a member, and the record includes no membership cards. The director's RFE noted this criterion requires evidence of membership in an association and evidence of the requirements for that membership, but petitioner did not respond. On appeal, counsel no longer asserted the petitioner meets this criterion. The AAO concurred with the director that petitioner had not submitted any evidence relating to this criterion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted: (1) articles in the New York Times, an unidentified newspaper, and the Portland, Maine Evening Express (discussed under awards); (2) a 2000 article in the Boston Globe reviewing a concert at the New England Conservatory of Daniel Pinkham's work, a three-column article with a single sentence praising petitioner's piano ability; (3) another three-column Boston Globe concert review mentioning petitioner in one sentence where she accompanied a soprano singing 'The Twilight Stood' (other accompanists also mentioned); (4) a 1993 concert review in New York Concert Review regarding petitioner's solo performance at Weill Recital Hall at Carnegie Hall; (5) a full-page invitation to Pianofest; (6) an 'opinion' piece in an unidentified paper reviewing a student recital, mentioning petitioner in one paragraph of a three-column piece.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found the materials failed to meet the criterion. The regulation at 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii) expressly requires published material 'about the alien' relating to her work — not merely published material about the alien's work (compare 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(i)(3)(i)(C) for outstanding professors/researchers). Three-column articles containing only a sentence or paragraph about the petitioner cannot be considered published material about her; they are primarily about the event at which she and many others played. While the reviews praise her piano ability, they are not primarily about her. Some articles are undated, and petitioner did not identify the publication for one article and the opinion piece, failing to comply with 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(iii). The Pianofest invitation bears no indicia of publication. While the New York Concert Review article and invitation focus on petitioner, the record lacks evidence that New York Concert Review has national circulation, and the 1993 article is dated 14 years before the petition was filed and cannot establish sustained acclaim as of the 2007 filing date. The director also noted the materials were not primarily about the petitioner and required data confirming publication circulation.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel initially asserted petitioner's teaching experience serves to meet this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded that while a teacher 'judges' her students, the duties inherent to every teacher cannot serve to set the alien apart from others in the field. Counsel no longer asserted this criterion on appeal. The AAO concurred with the director's analysis and conclusion.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted reference letters from: (1) a timpanist with the San Francisco Symphony who played with petitioner at the Pacific Music Festival, stating she was 'the first musician ever who devised a way to play on the piano the orchestral reduction of Robert Kraft's Timpani Concerto' and that her performance 'is now used by other musicians worldwide as the new standard to interpret this musical piece' (letter contained the phrase 'please insert date'); (2) another collaborator who played with her in 2005 at the Pacific Music Festival stating she performed a difficult timpani concerto, had only a week to learn the orchestra reduction score, and made her own musical additions, and that the performance 'set up the standards on which the current performances of this timpani concert are judged now nationally and internationally'; (3) a letter discussing her adaptation and implementation in teaching of Eurhythmics combined with Harmonic Analysis, claiming her original approach to structural analysis 'has impacted how music and harmonies are taught and it is now widely applied'; (4) a letter from a Senior Lecturer in Music at MIT asserting her performances are 'artistic contributions of major significance in the field of music' due to her 'extraordinary musical qualities'; (5) letter from principal oboist with the Chicago Symphony Orchestra.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found this criterion unmet. Solicited letters cannot serve to meet this criterion in the absence of unsolicited independent evidence. The phrase 'please insert date' in one letter suggests it was prepared for the signer's signature rather than authored by him. Neither letter identifies specific independent musical groups who have adopted petitioner's orchestral reduction score. Going on record without supporting documentary evidence is insufficient per Matter of Soffici. The record contains no letters from a sample of musicians worldwide confirming use of her orchestral reduction score, no evidence petitioner published this score or that it sold widely, and no requests from independent musicians for a copy. Regarding the Eurhythmics teaching approach, no single independent institution utilizing her methods is identified, and there is no evidence of published articles/books, invitations to lecture to music teachers, letters from independent music professors using these approaches, or requests for information from independent professors. Mere talent in the field cannot meet this criterion without evidence of the impact of the alien's work nationwide or worldwide. While the AAO did not question the sincerity and expertise of the petitioner's collaborators, teachers, and MIT colleagues, their general assertions of talent and original contributions are insufficient without corroboration.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Petitioner submitted programs for numerous recitals and concerts as evidence to meet this criterion.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The director concluded this criterion applies to the visual arts, not the performing arts. Counsel no longer asserted petitioner meets this criterion on appeal. The AAO concurred that this criterion applies to visual arts, and counsel did not explain how petitioner's performances are comparable under 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(4) to an exclusive artistic exhibition designed to showcase a visual artist's work.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: Counsel asserted petitioner meets this criterion through: (1) her role for the Pacific Music Festival in Japan; (2) every venue at which she has performed; (3) her work as a teacher. Petitioner submitted a letter from the Secretariat General of the Pacific Music Festival Organizing Committee asserting the festival 'has welcomed more than 59 international pianists from 14 different countries; [the petitioner] was the only one who was invited back due to her extraordinary musical ability.' The letter claimed the festival was founded by a prominent figure and is one of the most prestigious international music festivals.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted that 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(3)(viii) requires evidence of a leading or critical role for a distinguished organization or establishment. Playing a critical role as an accompanist for a distinguished individual or event does not fit within this criterion. An event is not an organization or establishment. The AAO cannot conclude that every musician who performs at an event at a distinguished venue plays a leading or critical role for the sponsoring organization. The programs in the record reveal that another pianist, Timothy Bozarth, also played at more than one festival (listed as pianist in both 1995 and 1999), contradicting the Secretariat's claim that petitioner was the only one invited back. The record includes no promotional materials or media coverage of the festival to establish its distinguished reputation. Even if the Pacific Music Festival were considered distinguished and petitioner's role critical, she would only meet one criterion and falls far short on all others.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, upholding the director's denial. Review of the record did not establish that petitioner distinguished herself as a pianist to such an extent that she achieved sustained national or international acclaim or is within the small percentage at the very top of her field. While the evidence indicates petitioner shows talent as a pianist, it is not persuasive that her achievements set her significantly above almost all others in her field. The AAO also declined to consider new evidence submitted on appeal that addressed concerns raised in the RFE because petitioner failed to respond to the RFE despite being put on notice of evidentiary deficiencies. The petitioner failed to meet any of the regulatory criteria and therefore did not establish eligibility under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Act.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Matter of Caron International, 19 I&N Dec. 791 (Comm'r. 1988)
Matter of Treasure Craft of California, 14 I&N Dec. 190
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Procedural issues: (1) The director issued a Request for Evidence (RFE) on May 23, 2007, sent to counsel at her current address, but received no response. Rather than denying for abandonment under 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(13), the director denied on the merits. (2) The AAO declined to consider new evidence submitted on appeal that addressed concerns raised in the RFE, citing Matter of Soriano and Matter of Obaigbena, since petitioner had been put on notice of deficiencies and failed to respond. (3) The AAO noted a typographical error in the director's decision where the word 'not' appeared to be missing from the sentence regarding the awards criterion burden of proof. (4) One reference letter contained the phrase 'please insert date,' suggesting it was prepared for the signer's signature rather than originally authored by him, raising concerns about the letter's authenticity as independent expert testimony.
